main_summary.summary: Courtland Cox, interviewed by Joe Mosnier on July 8, 2011, at the Library of Congress Jefferson Building, recounts his trajectory from Harlem birth in 1941 and a 1945-1952 childhood in Trinidad through Catholic school in the Bronx, Howard University, and the founding of the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) with Ed Brown and Stokely Carmichael. Cox describes NAG's Project Awareness programming at Howard (the 1961 Bayard Rustin-Malcolm X debate moderated by Emmett Dorsey, the Norman Thomas thermonuclear-warfare debate, and the James Baldwin-Ossie Davis-John Oliver Killens symposium with Sterling A. Brown), the group's role as Julius W. Hobson's shock troops in Washington and on Route 40, and Howard NAG's differentiation from Nashville-style nonviolence-as-philosophy. He recounts the 1962 Robert Kennedy office sit-in with Stokely Carmichael and Tom Kahn, the 1963 March on Washington dispute over John Lewis's speech, the 1964 Atlantic City MFDP credentials fight, and SNCC's 1965-1966 Lowndes County organizing for independent black political power with Jack Minnis's research, Mr. Black Man pamphlets, and John Hulett's sheriff candidacy. Cox then traces his 1967 Stockholm participation in the Bertrand Russell war-crimes tribunal alongside Jean-Paul Sartre and Simone de Beauvoir, his role as Secretary-General of the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania under Julius Nyerere with C.L.R. James as intellectual godfather, the founding of Drum and Spear Bookstore + Drum and Spear Press with Charlie Cobb, Judy Richardson, and Anne and Marvin Holloway, and his reflections on King's assassination, the 14th Street rebellion under Mayor Walter E. Washington, and the unresolved economic dimension of the freedom struggle.
key_themes: Howard NAG and SNCC student organizing
main_summary.historical_significance:
Cox is one of the few surviving SNCC central-committee figures who connected Howard University student organizing, southern field work in Lowndes County, the Atlantic City MFDP delegation, and the post-1968 turn toward Pan-African political economy. His firsthand account of the John Lewis March on Washington speech negotiation (with Jim Forman and A. Philip Randolph behind the Lincoln Memorial), the Bob Moses-Bob list incident at Atlantic City, and his role as Secretary-General organizing the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania places him at the hinge points where SNCC's faith in federal-rules legitimacy collapsed and where the movement reoriented toward Black Power, international alliance, and economic infrastructure.

The interview is also a rare source for the 1961 Cramton Auditorium Rustin-Malcolm X debate (moderated by Howard government professor Emmett Dorsey), the early NAG-Nashville philosophical divide over nonviolence as tactic versus way of life, and the McComb FBI-protect-evidence-not-people exchange that shaped SNCC's view of federal inaction. Cox's testimony on FBI, CIA, and IRS surveillance after his Bertrand Russell tribunal participation, including his 1970 detention at Heathrow, documents the intelligence-state response to internationalist movement leadership.

chapters.title: Trinidad and Bronx Childhood
chapters.summary: Cox describes being born in Harlem in 1941, moving to Trinidad with his mother and sister Lorraine in 1945, and returning to New York in 1952 after his grandmother's death. He recounts attending St. Helena's and St. Aloysius Catholic schools in the Bronx, the family expectation of college from his Trinidadian cousins with PhDs, and hiding his books walking home through housing-project peer groups affected by drugs.

chapters.title: Howard Admission and Early Awareness
chapters.summary: Cox follows his cousin Erskine to Howard University, taking a Greyhound bus to Washington to sit the entrance exam at a time when tuition was $7.50 per semester credit. He describes his pre-Howard political awareness shaped by Emmett Till and Montgomery and the older jazz-scene mentors who introduced him to Black culture in the Bronx.

chapters.title: NAG and Project Awareness
chapters.summary: Cox describes encountering Washington's segregation and forming the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) at Howard with Stokely Carmichael and others. He details NAG's Glen Echo Park, Eastern Shore, RFK Stadium, and Route 40 actions, work as Julius W. Hobson's CORE shock troops, and Project Awareness programming including the Bayard Rustin-Malcolm X debate, the Norman Thomas thermonuclear-warfare debate, and the James Baldwin-Ossie Davis-John Oliver Killens symposium moderated by Sterling A. Brown with Sidney Poitier flying in for the after-party. Tom Kahn and Mike Thelwell are named as student-council and newspaper leaders.

chapters.title: SNCC and Howard vs Nashville
chapters.summary: Cox describes NAG joining SNCC as one of several campus coordinating groups, his role on SNCC's coordinating council, the long Atlanta drives down Highway 29, and the SNCC discussion culture that moved from 'why' to 'why not.' He distinguishes Howard NAG's view of nonviolence as tactic from the Nashville group (Diane Nash, James Lawson, John Lewis) view of nonviolence as a way of life, arguing that movement participants engaged in nonviolence because the other side had overwhelming force, not because power could be reasoned with.

chapters.title: Rustin-Malcolm Debate and Federal Limits
chapters.summary: Cox describes the 1961 Cramton Auditorium debate between Bayard Rustin and Malcolm X, moderated by Howard government professor Emmett Dorsey after the government department chair refused. He recounts Rustin yielding fifteen minutes to Malcolm and the discipline of Malcolm's followers. Cox then describes the McComb FBI agent who told SNCC organizers the bureau was there to protect evidence, not people, and admitted having two guns and being scared.

chapters.title: Robert Kennedy Office Sit-In
chapters.summary: Cox describes the 1962 sit-in at Robert Kennedy's office with Stokely Carmichael and Tom Kahn. He recounts Kahn calling Drum and Spear Bookstore + Drum and Spear Press contacts and the Soviet press from Kennedy's phone, the dragging-out of limp protesters down the hall, and Stokely going back to retrieve forgotten items and sitting down again. Cox frames the Kennedy administration's posture toward Martin Luther King and toward SNCC as opportunistic.

chapters.title: March on Washington and John Lewis Speech
chapters.summary: Cox, the SNCC representative on the March on Washington steering committee, describes circulating John Lewis's original draft speech, Archbishop O'Boyle's threat to withdraw over criticism of the Kennedy administration and the 'march through the South like Sherman' line, and the late-night meeting behind the Lincoln Memorial with Jim Forman, John Lewis, and others where A. Philip Randolph appealed to the coalition he had worked since 1941 to build. Cox and Forman were criticized within SNCC for caving.

chapters.title: Atlantic City and the MFDP Betrayal
chapters.summary: Cox describes the 1964 MFDP delegation arriving at Atlantic City having played by the Democratic Party rules and finding the rules changed. He recounts Lyndon Johnson calling a press conference to cut off Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, his and Bob Moses's work with Rep. Edith Green to build credentials-committee support, Johnson's threat to deny Hubert Humphrey the vice presidency, and an unnamed congressman taking the supporters list from Bob Moses and using it to break the minority. The two-seat compromise was rejected; the liberal establishment including the National Council of Churches, AFL-CIO, Martin Luther King, NAACP, and Joseph L. Rauh Jr. urged acceptance.

chapters.title: Lowndes County and Independent Power
chapters.summary: Cox describes Lowndes County, Alabama as 80% Black with four registered voters when SNCC arrived after Viola Liuzzo's murder. He recounts Jack Minnis's research uncovering the Alabama law permitting county-level political parties with the rooster as the Democratic Party symbol, the comic books designed to communicate the roles and responsibilities of county offices to people who did not read, and the 'Mr. Black Man' propaganda piece he developed with Jennifer Lawson using a Mississippi woman's phrase about Black people using their mouths to eat and say yes. John Hulett ran for sheriff; Mr. Jackson housed the SNCC organizers and stood guard at night with his gun.

chapters.title: Violence and Living in the County
chapters.summary: Cox describes the SNCC organizers' rationalizations for managing fear in Lowndes County, the Collie Leroy Wilkins Jr. trial for Viola Liuzzo's murder where Cox was the only person of color in the courtroom, the chase down dirt roads at 70-80 miles per hour with John Jackson (Mr. Jackson's son), and the house with no running water and a hole in the roof. SNCC's trademark, Cox argues, was that organizers lived where they worked rather than dropping in and out.

chapters.title: Black Power and Whites in SNCC
chapters.summary: Cox describes the 1966 transition from John Lewis to Stokely Carmichael, the Meredith March Against Fear and the Black Power slogan, and the Peg Leg Bates Country Club staff meeting decision about white organizers. He recounts Jim Forman's framing that the problem was not in the Black community but in the white community, and that white organizers should organize in white communities. Theodore Bikel's public reaction cut SNCC funding tremendously.
related_events: Peg Leg Bates Country Club staff meeting

chapters.title: Stockholm and the Russell Tribunal
chapters.summary: Cox describes traveling to London and Stockholm in 1967 as SNCC's representative on Bertrand Russell's International War Crimes Tribunal. He sits at the dinner with Russell, Jean-Paul Sartre, Simone de Beauvoir, and Isaac Deutscher, observes Sartre's reverence in the proceedings, and questions Colonel Ha Van Lau about cluster bombs and guerrilla warfare, arguing that an industrialized power could only fight a war of national liberation by committing genocide. Cox details the resulting FBI, CIA, and IRS surveillance and his 1970 two-night detention at Heathrow en route to Africa.

chapters.title: Sixth Pan-African Congress
chapters.summary: Cox describes meeting C.L.R. James and Paulo (Carmody) and the conception of the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania to connect African-descended people across the continent and diaspora around the technical-skills-for-resources exchange and support for liberation movements. As Secretary-General at age 32, Cox traveled with C.L.R. James and Fletcher Robinson to meet Ahmed Sekou Toure of Guinea, Forbes Burnham of Guyana, and Julius Nyerere of Tanzania. State-versus-insurgent tensions limited Caribbean and Eritrean participation; Charles J. Ogletree Jr. attended. The conference lacked post-event infrastructure.

chapters.title: Drum and Spear and 1968
chapters.summary: Cox describes settling in Washington after the Russell Tribunal and founding Drum and Spear Bookstore + Drum and Spear Press at 14th and Fairmont Street with Charlie Cobb, Judy Richardson, and Anne and Marvin Holloway, alongside the Center for Black Education. He recounts hearing of Martin Luther King's assassination in a Volkswagen en route to Pennsylvania, the 14th Street rebellion, the internal solidarity among Black participants versus external hostility, and Mayor Walter E. Washington declaring martial law with tanks on Columbia Road. Cox closes by reflecting on Bobby Kennedy's voice after King's death and the Chicago Convention.

chapters.title: Economic Continuum and Lessons
chapters.summary: Cox frames the civil-rights era from 1955 to 2008 as the political era, with three accomplishments: ending public-accommodations segregation, securing voting rights, and building electoral political organization. He argues that what was radical was not the tactics but who was included, and that the unresolved task is the economic dimension. The information economy requires literacy in English, math, and computers; the inclusion now must extend to poor whites and Native Americans, with intellectual property as the substrate for wealth and institution-building.
